markdown.markdown:
These are the five extensions I install immediately on any new VS Code setup. No bloat, just the essentials.
## 1. ESLint
The cornerstone of any JavaScript/TypeScript project. Catches bugs, enforces patterns, and auto-fixes on save.
```json
// settings.json
{
  "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
    "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit"
  }
}
```
## 2. Prettier
Automatic code formatting. No more arguing about semicolons or tab width — just hit save.
```json
{
  "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode",
  "editor.formatOnSave": true
}
```
## 3. GitLens
Inline git blame, file history, and commit visualization. The killer feature: hovering over any line shows who last changed it and when. Invaluable for understanding *why* code looks the way it does.
## 4. CSS Peek
Ctrl+click on a class name in HTML to jump to its CSS definition. Also works in JSX. Small but saves a surprising amount of time when working with stylesheets.
## 5. Error Lens
Shows errors and warnings **inline** next to the problematic line, instead of only in the Problems panel. This makes issues impossible to miss:
- Red highlight for errors
- Yellow for warnings
- Blue for info
You catch problems the moment you type them, not when you scroll through a list.
---
Honorable mentions: **Auto Rename Tag** (syncs opening/closing HTML tags), **TODO Highlight** (makes TODO comments visible), and **Import Cost** (shows the bundle size of imported packages inline).
